review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the order of calls in __split_vma, vma_shrink, and commit_merge, which can lead to a huge PMD straddling at VMA boundary if split_huge_pmd_if_needed() fails. The author moved the call to vma_adjust_trans_huge() before vma_prepare(), ensuring that a split failure returns -ENOMEM cleanly without needing to undo VMA preparation.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the behavior of copy_huge_pmd() when splitting a PMD fails, explaining that returning -EAGAIN would cause incorrect dereferencing of the huge PMD entry as if it were a PTE page table pointer. Instead, they will return -ENOMEM on split failure to abort the fork with an error code similar to other failure cases.
review_comments.raw_body:
copy_huge_pmd() splits the source PMD when a folio is pinned and can't
be COW-shared at PMD granularity.  It then returns -EAGAIN so
copy_pmd_range() falls through to copy_pte_range().

If the split fails, the PMD is still huge.  Returning -EAGAIN would cause
copy_pmd_range() to call copy_pte_range(), which would dereference the
huge PMD entry as if it were a pointer to a PTE page table.
Return -ENOMEM on split failure instead (which is already done in
copy_huge_pmd() if pte_alloc_one() fails), which causes copy_page_range()
to abort the fork with -ENOMEM, similar to how copy_pmd_range() would
be aborted if pmd_alloc() and copy_pte_range() fail.

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/huge_memory.c | 8 +++++++-
 1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
index a979aa5bd2995..d9fb5875fa59e 100644
--- a/mm/huge_memory.c
+++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
@@ -1929,7 +1929,13 @@ int copy_huge_pmd(struct mm_struct *dst_mm, struct mm_struct *src_mm,
 		pte_free(dst_mm, pgtable);
 		spin_unlock(src_ptl);
 		spin_unlock(dst_ptl);
-		__split_huge_pmd(src_vma, src_pmd, addr, false);
+		/*
+		 * If split fails, the PMD is still huge so copy_pte_range
+		 * (via -EAGAIN) would misinterpret it as a page table
+		 * pointer.  Return -ENOMEM directly to copy_pmd_range.
+		 */
+		if (__split_huge_pmd(src_vma, src_pmd, addr, false))
+			return -ENOMEM;
 		return -EAGAIN;
 	}
 	add_mm_counter(dst_mm, MM_ANONPAGES, HPAGE_PMD_NR);
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the behavior of do_huge_pmd_wp_page() when splitting a PMD fails, explaining that returning VM_FAULT_FALLBACK would cause incorrect behavior and proposing to return VM_FAULT_OOM instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
do_huge_pmd_wp_page() splits the PMD when a COW of the entire huge page
fails (e.g., can't allocate a new folio or the folio is pinned).  It then
returns VM_FAULT_FALLBACK so the fault can be retried at PTE granularity.

If the split fails, the PMD is still huge.  Returning VM_FAULT_FALLBACK
would re-enter the PTE fault path, which expects a PTE page table at the
PMD entry \u2014 not a huge PMD.

Return VM_FAULT_OOM on split failure, which signals the fault handler to
invoke the OOM killer or return -ENOMEM to userspace.

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/huge_memory.c | 8 +++++++-
 1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
index d9fb5875fa59e..e82b8435a0b7f 100644
--- a/mm/huge_memory.c
+++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
@@ -2153,7 +2153,13 @@ vm_fault_t do_huge_pmd_wp_page(struct vm_fault *vmf)
 	folio_unlock(folio);
 	spin_unlock(vmf->ptl);
 fallback:
-	__split_huge_pmd(vma, vmf->pmd, vmf->address, false);
+	/*
+	 * Split failure means the PMD is still huge; returning
+	 * VM_FAULT_FALLBACK would re-enter the PTE path with a
+	 * huge PMD, causing incorrect behavior.
+	 */
+	if (__split_huge_pmd(vma, vmf->pmd, vmf->address, false))
+		return VM_FAULT_OOM;
 	return VM_FAULT_FALLBACK;
 }
 
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about zap_pmd_range() handling split failures by skipping the range covered by the PMD on split failure instead of falling through to zap_pte_range(). Author explained that this is safe across all call paths into zap_pmd_range(), citing specific scenarios where the split failure path is unreachable. No fix is planned for this patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
zap_pmd_range() splits a huge PMD when the zap range doesn't cover the
full PMD (partial unmap).  If the split fails, the PMD stays huge.
Falling through to zap_pte_range() would dereference the huge PMD entry
as a PTE page table pointer.

Skip the range covered by the PMD on split failure instead.

The skip is safe across all call paths into zap_pmd_range():

- exit_mmap() and OOM reaper: the zap range covers entire VMAs, so
  every PMD is fully covered (next - addr == HPAGE_PMD_SIZE).  The
  zap_huge_pmd() branch handles these without splitting.  The split
  failure path is unreachable.

- munmap / mmap overlay: vma_adjust_trans_huge() (called from
  __split_vma) splits any PMD straddling the VMA boundary before the
  VMA is split.  If that PMD split fails, __split_vma() returns
  -ENOMEM and the munmap is aborted before reaching zap_pmd_range().
  The split failure path is unreachable.

- MADV_DONTNEED: advisory hint, the kernel is allowed to ignore it.
  The pages remain valid and accessible.  A subsequent access returns
  existing data without faulting.

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/memory.c | 15 ++++++++++++---
 1 file changed, 12 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
index 9385842c35034..7ba1221c63792 100644
--- a/mm/memory.c
+++ b/mm/memory.c
@@ -1983,9 +1983,18 @@ static inline unsigned long zap_pmd_range(struct mmu_gather *tlb,
 	do {
 		next = pmd_addr_end(addr, end);
 		if (pmd_is_huge(*pmd)) {
-			if (next - addr != HPAGE_PMD_SIZE)
-				__split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, addr, false);
-			else if (zap_huge_pmd(tlb, vma, pmd, addr)) {
+			if (next - addr != HPAGE_PMD_SIZE) {
+				/*
+				 * If split fails, the PMD stays huge.
+				 * Skip the range to avoid falling through
+				 * to zap_pte_range, which would treat the
+				 * huge PMD entry as a page table pointer.
+				 */
+				if (__split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, addr, false)) {
+					addr = next;
+					continue;
+				}
+			} else if (zap_huge_pmd(tlb, vma, pmd, addr)) {
 				addr = next;
 				continue;
 			}
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the behavior of wp_huge_pmd() when splitting PMDs for COW or write-notify, explaining that it returns VM_FAULT_OOM on split failure to signal the OOM killer or return -ENOMEM to userspace. The author confirmed this is similar to what __handle_mm_fault would do if p4d_alloc or pud_alloc fails.
review_comments.raw_body:
wp_huge_pmd() splits the PMD when COW or write-notify must be handled at
PTE level (e.g., shared/file VMAs, userfaultfd).  It then returns
VM_FAULT_FALLBACK so the fault handler retries at PTE granularity.
If the split fails, the PMD is still huge.  The PTE fault path cannot
handle a huge PMD entry.
Return VM_FAULT_OOM on split failure, which signals the fault handler to
invoke the OOM killer or return -ENOMEM to userspace. This is similar to
what __handle_mm_fault would do if p4d_alloc or pud_alloc fails.

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/memory.c | 9 +++++++--
 1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
index 7ba1221c63792..51d2717e3f1b4 100644
--- a/mm/memory.c
+++ b/mm/memory.c
@@ -6161,8 +6161,13 @@ static inline vm_fault_t wp_huge_pmd(struct vm_fault *vmf)
 	}
 
 split:
-	/* COW or write-notify handled on pte level: split pmd. */
-	__split_huge_pmd(vma, vmf->pmd, vmf->address, false);
+	/*
+	 * COW or write-notify handled on pte level: split pmd.
+	 * If split fails, the PMD is still huge so falling back
+	 * to PTE handling would be incorrect.
+	 */
+	if (__split_huge_pmd(vma, vmf->pmd, vmf->address, false))
+		return VM_FAULT_OOM;
 
 	return VM_FAULT_FALLBACK;
 }
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that __split_huge_pmd() returning an error code would cause issues in change_protection_range(). They explained that the huge PMD remains intact if pte_alloc_one() fails, and proposed handling this by yielding the CPU with cond_resched() and retrying via goto again. The author confirmed that trying to return an error all the way to change_protection_range() is not feasible.
review_comments.raw_body:
change_pmd_range() splits a huge PMD when mprotect() targets a sub-PMD
range or when VMA flags require per-PTE protection bits that can't be
represented at PMD granularity.

If pte_alloc_one() fails inside __split_huge_pmd(), the huge PMD remains
intact. Without this change, change_pte_range() would return -EAGAIN
because pte_offset_map_lock() returns NULL for a huge PMD, sending the
code back to the 'again' label to retry the split\u2014without ever calling
cond_resched().

Now that __split_huge_pmd() returns an error code, handle it explicitly:
yield the CPU with cond_resched() and retry via goto again, giving other
tasks a chance to free memory.

Trying to return an error all the way to change_protection_range would
not work as it would leave a memory range with new protections, and
others unchanged, with no easy way to roll back the already modified
entries (and previous splits). __split_huge_pmd only requires an
order-0 allocation and is extremely unlikely to fail.

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/mprotect.c | 11 ++++++++++-
 1 file changed, 10 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/mm/mprotect.c b/mm/mprotect.c
index 9681f055b9fca..599d80a7d6969 100644
--- a/mm/mprotect.c
+++ b/mm/mprotect.c
@@ -477,7 +477,16 @@ static inline long change_pmd_range(struct mmu_gather *tlb,
 		if (pmd_is_huge(_pmd)) {
 			if ((next - addr != HPAGE_PMD_SIZE) ||
 			    pgtable_split_needed(vma, cp_flags)) {
-				__split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, addr, false);
+				ret = __split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, addr, false);
+				if (ret) {
+					/*
+					 * Yield and retry. Other tasks
+					 * may free memory while we
+					 * reschedule.
+					 */
+					cond_resched();
+					goto again;
+				}
 				/*
 				 * For file-backed, the pmd could have been
 				 * cleared; make sure pmd populated if
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that follow_pmd_mask() should return an error pointer on split failure, explaining it's safe because -ENOMEM is already returned in this case and the PMD will remain huge if split fails.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the split failure in walk_pmd_range() needs to be handled by breaking out of the loop and returning -ENOMEM to the walker's caller, agreed to make this change.
review_comments.raw_body:
walk_pmd_range() splits a huge PMD when a page table walker with
pte_entry or install_pte callbacks needs PTE-level granularity. If
the split fails due to memory allocation failure in pte_alloc_one(),
walk_pte_range() would encounter a huge PMD instead of a PTE page
table.

Break out of the loop on split failure and return -ENOMEM to the
walker's caller. Callers that reach this path (those with pte_entry
or install_pte set) such as mincore, hmm_range_fault and
queue_pages_range already handle negative return values from
walk_page_range(). Similar approach is taken when __pte_alloc()
fails in walk_pmd_range().

Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
---
 mm/pagewalk.c | 8 +++++---
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)

diff --git a/mm/pagewalk.c b/mm/pagewalk.c
index a94c401ab2cfe..1ee9df7a4461d 100644
--- a/mm/pagewalk.c
+++ b/mm/pagewalk.c
@@ -147,9 +147,11 @@ static int walk_pmd_range(pud_t *pud, unsigned long addr, unsigned long end,
 				continue;
 		}
 
-		if (walk->vma)
-			split_huge_pmd(walk->vma, pmd, addr);
-		else if (pmd_leaf(*pmd) || !pmd_present(*pmd))
+		if (walk->vma) {
+			err = split_huge_pmd(walk->vma, pmd, addr);
+			if (err)
+				break;
+		} else if (pmd_leaf(*pmd) || !pmd_present(*pmd))
 			continue; /* Nothing to do. */
 
 		err = walk_pte_range(pmd, addr, next, walk);
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the kernel test bot reported a missed split_huge_pmd call and promised to include a patch in the next revision to handle this case, specifically by checking the return value of split_huge_pmd() and propagating the error on failure.
review_comments.raw_body:
Kernel test bot reported that I missed one split_huge_pmd call. I will include
the below patch in the next revision.


commit 9e1bb250ea8ef0a39c738cd4137ed6c98131ebb0 (HEAD)
Author: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Date:   Thu Feb 26 10:45:35 2026 -0800

    mm: proc: handle split_huge_pmd failure in pagemap_scan
    
    pagemap_scan_thp_entry() splits a huge PMD when the PAGEMAP_SCAN ioctl
    needs to write-protect only a portion of a THP. It then returns -ENOENT
    so pagemap_scan_pmd_entry() falls through to PTE-level handling.
    
    Check the split_huge_pmd() return value and propagate the error on
    failure. Returning -ENOMEM instead of -ENOENT prevents the fallthrough
    to PTE handling, and the error propagates through walk_page_range() to
    do_pagemap_scan() where it becomes the ioctl return value.
    pagemap_scan_backout_range() already undoes the buffered output, and
    walk_end is written back to userspace so the caller knows where the
    scan stopped.
    
    If the split fails, the PMD remains huge. An alternative to the approach
    in the patch is to return -ENOENT, causing the caller to proceed to
    pte_offset_map_lock(). ___pte_offset_map() detects the trans_huge PMD
    and returns NULL, which sets ACTION_AGAIN \u2014 restarting the walker on the
    same PMD by which time the system might have enough memory to satisfy
    the split from succeeding.
    
    Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

diff --git a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
index e091931d7ca19..f5f459140b5c0 100644
--- a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
+++ b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
@@ -2714,9 +2714,13 @@ static int pagemap_scan_thp_entry(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long start,
         * needs to be performed on a portion of the huge page.
         */
        if (end != start + HPAGE_SIZE) {
+               int err;
+
                spin_unlock(ptl);
-               split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, start);
+               err = split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, start);
                pagemap_scan_backout_range(p, start, end);
+               if (err)
+                       return err;
                /* Report as if there was no THP */
                return -ENOENT;
        }

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about refcounting issues in the split_huge_pmd_address function when it fails, explained that folio_put is needed to decrement the reference count, and confirmed they will add this fix.
sentiment_signals: confirmed they will add this fix
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Nico,

Thanks for pointing this out. It spun out to an entire investigation for me [1].

Similar to [1], I inserted trace prints [2] and created a new __split_huge_pmd2
that always returns -ENOMEM. Without folio_put on error [3], we get a refcount of 2.

       hmm-tests-129     [000] .l...     1.485514: __migrate_device_finalize: FINALIZE[0]: src=ffb48827440e8000 dst=ffb48827440e8000 src==dst=1 refcount_src=2 mapcount_src=0 order_src=9 migrate=0 BEFORE remove_migration_ptes
       hmm-tests-129     [000] .l...     1.485517: __migrate_device_finalize: FINALIZE[0]: src=ffb48827440e8000 refcount=3 mapcount=1 AFTER remove_migration_ptes
       hmm-tests-129     [000] .l...     1.485518: __migrate_device_finalize: FINALIZE[0]: src=ffb48827440e8000 refcount=2 AFTER folio_put(src)


With folio_put on error [4], we get a refcount of 1.

       hmm-tests-129     [001] .....     1.492216: __migrate_device_finalize: FINALIZE[0]: src=fff7b8be840f0000 dst=fff7b8be840f0000 src==dst=1 refcount_src=1 mapcount_src=0 order_src=9 migrate=0 BEFORE remove_migration_ptes
       hmm-tests-129     [001] .....     1.492219: __migrate_device_finalize: FINALIZE[0]: src=fff7b8be840f0000 refcount=2 mapcount=1 AFTER remove_migration_ptes
       hmm-tests-129     [001] .....     1.492220: __migrate_device_finalize: FINALIZE[0]: src=fff7b8be840f0000 refcount=1 AFTER folio_put(src)


So we need folio_put for split_huge_pmd_address failure, but NOT for
folio_split_unmapped.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 01/21] mm: thp: make split_huge_pmd functions return int for error propagation
series_items.patch_summary: THP_SPLIT_PMD_FAILED counter patch requires CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE guard and additional diff in a future series.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Identified a need for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE guard and additional diff in a future series. Requested author to address these concerns.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 02/21] mm: thp: propagate split failure from vma_adjust_trans_huge()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes changes to the Transparent Huge Pages (THP) functionality in the Linux kernel. The main goal is to propagate split failure from vma_adjust_trans_huge() to prevent huge PMD straddling at VMA boundaries. This is achieved by moving the vma_adjust_trans_huge() call earlier in three callers (__split_vma, vma_shrink, commit_merge) to allow for clean -ENOMEM returns in case of allocation failures.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the proposed change, suggesting that it might not be safe to move vma_adjust_trans_huge() earlier in the callers. Proposed an alternative solution.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 03/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in copy_huge_pmd()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes changes to the Transparent Huge Pages (THP) handling in the Linux kernel, specifically addressing a split failure scenario in the copy_huge_pmd() function. The goal is to return -ENOMEM on split failure instead of -EAGAIN, preventing potential dereferences of huge PMD entries.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's impact on existing code and suggested a more comprehensive approach to handling split failures. Requested additional changes before considering it for merge.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 04/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in do_huge_pmd_wp_page()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch proposes a change to the handling of split failures in do_huge_pmd_wp_page(). When a huge page is split due to a COW failure, it currently returns VM_FAULT_FALLBACK, which can lead to incorrect behavior. The proposed change would return VM_FAULT_OOM instead, signaling the fault handler to invoke the OOM killer or return -ENOMEM.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the potential impact of returning VM_FAULT_OOM, suggesting that it might not be the best solution. Asked for more information and discussion.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 05/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in zap_pmd_range()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch proposes a change to the `zap_pmd_range()` function in the Linux kernel's memory management code. The function is responsible for handling partial unmaps of huge pages, and the patch aims to improve its behavior when splitting huge PMDs fails.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: No substantive review comments have been posted yet, but the patch has not been applied or merged by a maintainer. The thread is still in an active discussion phase.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 06/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in wp_huge_pmd()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch proposes a change to the wp_huge_pmd() function in the mm module, which handles split failures when handling COW or write-notify at PTE level. The current implementation returns VM_FAULT_FALLBACK on split failure, but this can lead to incorrect behavior if the PMD is still huge. The proposed change returns VM_FAULT_OOM instead, signaling the fault handler to invoke the OOM killer or return -ENOMEM.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the potential impact of returning VM_FAULT_OOM on split failure, suggesting that it might lead to incorrect behavior in certain scenarios. Asked for clarification on the proposed change.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 07/21] mm: thp: retry on split failure in change_pmd_range()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch proposes a change to the mm/mprotect.c file, specifically in the change_pmd_range() function. The goal is to handle split failures more robustly by retrying the operation after yielding the CPU and allowing other tasks to free memory.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 08/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in follow_pmd_mask()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a change to handle split failures in the follow_pmd_mask() function, which is responsible for splitting huge PMDs when FOLL_SPLIT_PMD is set. If the split fails, it returns an error pointer (-ENOMEM) to prevent further processing.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: No summary available as this is the original patch author's root message.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 09/21] mm: handle walk_page_range() failure from THP split
series_items.patch_summary: This patch proposes a change to the pagewalk code in the mm module, specifically handling failures when splitting huge PMDs during page table walks. The current implementation breaks out of the loop on split failure and returns -ENOMEM to the walker's caller.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's approach to handling split failures and suggested an alternative solution.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 10/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in mremap move_page_tables()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes handling split failures in the move_page_tables() function, which is responsible for splitting huge PMDs when they cannot be moved at PMD granularity. The current implementation breaks out of the loop on split failure and returns the amount of memory that was successfully moved.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the potential for double-put bugs when splitting huge PMDs. Suggested adding an extra folio_get() before replace_page_cache_folio() to maintain the reference.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 11/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in userfaultfd move_pages()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes handling split failures in the userfaultfd move_pages() function by breaking out of the loop and returning -ENOMEM to the caller when a huge PMD cannot be split.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the handling of split failures and suggested adding a check for the PMD being huge before attempting to split it.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 12/21] mm: thp: handle split failure in device migration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential issue in device memory migration where huge page splits can fail, leading to inconsistent page table states. The patch modifies two call sites to handle split failures: when splitting PMDs during migration and when inserting pages into VMAs after migration. If a split fails, the function returns an error code, preventing further operations on the unsplit folio or PMD slot.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 13/21] mm: huge_mm: Make sure all split_huge_pmd calls are checked
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the handling of huge page mappings in the Linux kernel by ensuring that all split_huge_pmd calls are checked for their return value. The current implementation does not check the return value, which can lead to kernel bugs if an order-0 allocation fails for page table allocation. The patch modifies several functions to mark __split_huge_pmd(), split_huge_pmd() and split_huge_pmd_address() with __must_check so that the compiler warns if any caller ignores the return value.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Responded to a kernel test bot report by mentioning that they will include a patch for one missed split_huge_pmd call in the next revision.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 26/02/2026 11:23, Usama Arif wrote:
> Mark __split_huge_pmd(), split_huge_pmd() and split_huge_pmd_address()
> with __must_check so the compiler warns if any caller ignores the return
> value. Not checking return value and operating on the basis that the pmd
> is split could result in a kernel bug. The possibility of an order-0
> allocation failing for page table allocation is very low, but it should
> be handled correctly.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>


Kernel test bot reported that I missed one split_huge_pmd call. I will include
the below patch in the next revision.


commit 9e1bb250ea8ef0a39c738cd4137ed6c98131ebb0 (HEAD)
Author: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
Date:   Thu Feb 26 10:45:35 2026 -0800

    mm: proc: handle split_huge_pmd failure in pagemap_scan
    
    pagemap_scan_thp_entry() splits a huge PMD when the PAGEMAP_SCAN ioctl
    needs to write-protect only a portion of a THP. It then returns -ENOENT
    so pagemap_scan_pmd_entry() falls through to PTE-level handling.
    
    Check the split_huge_pmd() return value and propagate the error on
    failure. Returning -ENOMEM instead of -ENOENT prevents the fallthrough
    to PTE handling, and the error propagates through walk_page_range() to
    do_pagemap_scan() where it becomes the ioctl return value.
    pagemap_scan_backout_range() already undoes the buffered output, and
    walk_end is written back to userspace so the caller knows where the
    scan stopped.
    
    If the split fails, the PMD remains huge. An alternative to the approach
    in the patch is to return -ENOENT, causing the caller to proceed to
    pte_offset_map_lock(). ___pte_offset_map() detects the trans_huge PMD
    and returns NULL, which sets ACTION_AGAIN \u2014 restarting the walker on the
    same PMD by which time the system might have enough memory to satisfy
    the split from succeeding.
    
    Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

diff --git a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
index e091931d7ca19..f5f459140b5c0 100644
--- a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
+++ b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
@@ -2714,9 +2714,13 @@ static int pagemap_scan_thp_entry(pmd_t *pmd, unsigned long start,
         * needs to be performed on a portion of the huge page.
         */
        if (end != start + HPAGE_SIZE) {
+               int err;
+
                spin_unlock(ptl);
-               split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, start);
+               err = split_huge_pmd(vma, pmd, start);
                pagemap_scan_backout_range(p, start, end);
+               if (err)
+                       return err;
                /* Report as if there was no THP */
                return -ENOENT;
        }

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 14/21] mm: thp: allocate PTE page tables lazily at split time
series_items.patch_summary: This patch proposes to allocate PTE page tables lazily at split time for anonymous pages, rather than pre-allocating them upfront. This change aims to reduce memory waste on systems with many THPs by avoiding the unnecessary allocation of unused page table pages.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 15/21] mm: thp: remove pgtable_trans_huge_{deposit/withdraw} when not needed
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes to remove the generic implementation of pgtable_trans_huge_{deposit/withdraw} in mm/pgtable-generic.c, as it is only needed for architectures where arch_needs_pgtable_deposit() returns true (currently powerpc hash MMU). The patch renames these functions to arch_pgtable_trans_huge_{deposit/withdraw} and replaces the extern declarations with static inline no-op stubs for the default case.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified that the patch only removes dead code for architectures where arch_needs_pgtable_deposit() returns true. Suggested checking if this condition is still met after the rename and removal.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 16/21] mm: thp: add THP_SPLIT_PMD_FAILED counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes adding a new vmstat counter to track PTE allocation failures during PMD split, which enables monitoring of split failures due to memory pressure after the lazy PTE page table allocation change.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Guarded the THP_SPLIT_PMD_FAILED counter with CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE and added a diff in the next series to address concerns.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 17/21] selftests/mm: add THP PMD split test infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes to add a test infrastructure for verifying the behavior of Transparent Huge Pages (THP) PMD splitting, specifically when using lazy PTE allocation. The test includes a fixture with PMD-aligned memory allocation, helper functions for reading vmstat counters, and a macro for logging and checking if the THP split has occurred.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 18/21] selftests/mm: add partial_mprotect test for change_pmd_range
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test for partial mprotect on Transparent Huge Pages (THP) to exercise the change_pmd_range() function, which splits PMDs and applies protection only to requested portions.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 19/21] selftests/mm: add partial_mlock test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new test to the selftests/mm suite, specifically targeting partial mlock functionality on Transparent Huge Pages (THP). The test exercises walk_page_range() with a subset of the THP, which should trigger a PMD split since mlock operates at page granularity.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the test's cleanup logic, suggesting a more robust approach to ensure proper PMD split handling.

series_items.subject: [RFC v2 20/21] selftests/mm: add partial_mremap test for move_page_tables
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new selftest to the mm module, specifically designed to test partial mremap functionality on transparent huge pages (THPs). The test verifies that partial mremap correctly splits the PMD, moves only the requested page, and preserves data integrity in both the moved region and the original mapping. This is achieved through a series of allocations, writes, and verifications.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: No comment provided by David Hildenbrand in the thread.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the selftests/mm suite, specifically targeting the MADV_DONTNEED functionality on THP (Transparent Huge Pages). The test verifies that MADV_DONTNEED correctly triggers a PMD split, discards only the requested page, and preserves data in surrounding pages.
